q: How do you make a heading in Markdown?
a: Prefix the line with `#` — one `#` per heading level, up to six (`######`). A space after the `#` is required in CommonMark. `#` is an `<h1>`, `##` an `<h2>`, and so on.
fs: One `#` per level, space after the hash, max six levels.
fj: I wrap it in big HTML tags.

followups.q: How do you make an H1/H2 without `#` (setext style)?
followups.a: Put `===` on the line below for H1 or `---` for H2. At least one `=` or `-` is needed; the count doesn't matter.

q: What is the 'setext' heading style?
a: Write the heading text on one line, then underline it with `===` (H1) or `---` (H2) on the very next line. Only H1 and H2 are supported this way.
fs: `===` → H1, `---` → H2; only two levels available.
fj: I can use `---` for any heading level.

q: How do you make text **bold** or *italic* in Markdown?
a: Wrap text in `**double asterisks**` or `__double underscores__` for bold. Use `*single asterisk*` or `_single underscore_` for italic. Mix them: `***bold italic***`.
fs: `**bold**`, `*italic*`, `***both***`.
fj: Use `<b>` and `<i>` tags.

q: How do you add ~~strikethrough~~ text in Markdown?
a: Wrap the text in `~~double tildes~~`. This is a GFM extension — it is not part of the base CommonMark spec and may not render on non-GFM parsers.
fs: `~~text~~` — GFM only, not standard CommonMark.
fj: Use `~single tilde~` like some other markup languages.

q: How do you write inline code in Markdown?
a: Surround the code with single backticks: `` `code` ``. The text inside is rendered in a monospace font and special characters are not interpreted as Markdown.
fs: Single backticks for inline code: `` `value` ``.
fj: Use single quotes or bold for code-looking text.

followups.q: What if the inline code itself contains a backtick?
followups.a: Use double backticks as the delimiter: ` `` use `backtick` here `` `. Add spaces inside the outer backticks if the span starts or ends with a backtick.

q: How do you write a fenced code block with a language hint?
a: Open with three backticks followed immediately by the language name (the 'info string'), then close with three backticks on their own line. The language hint enables syntax highlighting.
fs: ` ```python ` on the opening line sets syntax highlighting.
fj: Indent every line with 4 spaces — that's the old style and has no language hint.

followups.q: Can you use tildes instead of backticks for a code fence?
followups.a: Yes. Three or more tildes (`~~~`) also open a fenced block. The closing fence must use the same character and be at least as long as the opener.

q: How do you create an unordered (bulleted) list in Markdown?
a: Start each item with `- `, `* `, or `+ ` followed by a space. All three markers work; pick one and stay consistent within a list.
fs: `-`, `*`, or `+` + space before each item.
fj: Use `•` character or indent with tabs to make bullets.

q: How do you create an ordered (numbered) list in Markdown?
a: Start each item with a number followed by `.` and a space, e.g. `1. `. The rendered numbers increment automatically — the actual numbers you type don't matter (CommonMark always starts from the first number you write).
fs: Any `N. item` sequence works; the parser sets the count.
fj: I must number every item correctly or the list breaks.

followups.q: What starting number does CommonMark use for an ordered list?
followups.a: The first number in the source sets the start value. `3. item` renders as list item 3. Subsequent items increment from there regardless of what you wrote.

q: How do you nest a list inside another list?
a: Indent the sub-items with at least two spaces (CommonMark requires enough spaces to align the content past the parent marker). Four spaces is a safe standard.
fs: Indent sub-items 2-4 spaces under the parent item.
fj: Add a blank line and the items will auto-nest.

q: How do you add an inline link in Markdown?
a: Use `[link text](URL)`. Optionally add a title in quotes: `[text](URL "Title")`. The title shows as a tooltip on hover.
fs: `[text](url)` — square brackets then parentheses.
fj: Paste the URL directly and hope it auto-links.

followups.q: What is a reference-style link and when is it useful?
followups.a: Define the URL once at the bottom as `[label]: URL` and use `[text][label]` inline. Useful when the same URL appears many times or you want cleaner prose.

q: What is a reference-style link in Markdown?
a: Separate the URL definition from the text. Write `[text][id]` inline and `[id]: https://...` anywhere in the file (usually at the bottom). The `id` is case-insensitive.
fs: `[text][id]` + `[id]: url` definition elsewhere in the file.
fj: Reference links only work at the top of the file.

q: How do you embed an image in Markdown?
a: Use `![alt text](image-url)`. It's identical to a link but with a `!` prefix. The alt text is shown when the image fails to load and is read by screen readers.
fs: `![alt](url)` — bang prefix on a normal link.
fj: Use `` tag; Markdown has no image syntax.

q: How do you add a blockquote in Markdown?
a: Prefix each line with `> `. You can nest blockquotes with `>>`. Blockquotes can contain other Markdown elements like lists or bold text.
fs: `> ` at the start of each quoted line.
fj: Indent the paragraph with spaces to make it a quote.

lines: > Be the change you wish to see.

q: How do you draw a horizontal rule (thematic break) in Markdown?
a: Write three or more `---`, `***`, or `___` on their own line (with optional spaces between). A blank line before `---` is required to avoid it being parsed as a setext H2 heading.
fs: Three dashes, stars, or underscores on their own line.
fj: Just press Enter a bunch of times to visually separate sections.

q: How do you create a table in Markdown (GFM)?
a: Use pipes `|` to separate columns and a `|---|` separator row between the header and body. Colons in the separator control alignment: `|:---|` left, `|---:|` right, `|:---:|` center.
fs: Pipe-separated columns + `|---|` separator row; GFM extension.
fj: Tables aren't possible in Markdown — use HTML.

followups.q: Are Markdown tables part of CommonMark or GFM?
followups.a: Tables are a GFM extension, not part of CommonMark. The CommonMark spec deliberately omits tables; a GFM-aware parser (GitHub, GitLab, VS Code preview) is needed to render them.

q: How do you create a task list (checkbox list) in Markdown?
a: Use `- [ ]` for an unchecked item and `- [x]` for a checked item. This is a GFM extension. On GitHub, checked items render as ticked checkboxes.
fs: `- [ ]` unchecked, `- [x]` checked — GFM only.
fj: Use a regular list and type '✓' manually.

q: What is the difference between a soft line break and a hard line break in Markdown?
a:
A single newline in Markdown source is a *soft break* — it renders as a space, not a new line. A *hard break* forces a `
`: add two trailing spaces before Enter, or a backslash `\` at the end of the line (CommonMark).
fs: Soft break = space; hard break = two trailing spaces or backslash.
fj: Press Enter once to start a new line in the output.

q: How do you escape a special Markdown character like `*` or `#`?
a: Prefix the character with a backslash `\`. For example, `\*` renders as a literal asterisk instead of starting emphasis. Works for: `\`, `` ` ``, `*`, `_`, `{}`, `[]`, `()`, `#`, `+`, `-`, `.`, `!`.
fs: Backslash before the character: `\*`, `\_`, `\#`.
fj: Wrap it in backticks — that makes it code, not an escape.

q: What are autolinks in GFM?
a: GFM automatically turns bare URLs and email addresses into clickable links without any Markdown syntax. Just paste `https://example.com` and it becomes a link. Standard CommonMark requires `<angle-bracket>` syntax for autolinks.
fs: GFM auto-links bare URLs; CommonMark needs `<url>` syntax.
fj: You always need `[text](url)` syntax to make a link.

q: Can you use raw HTML inside Markdown?
a: Yes — CommonMark allows inline and block HTML. GFM disallows a small set of potentially dangerous tags (like `<script>`, `<style>`, `<iframe>`) — they are filtered on GitHub. Most presentational HTML like `<details>` works fine.
fs: HTML is allowed; GFM strips a few dangerous tags server-side.
fj: Markdown renders HTML as plain text — it never executes it.

q: What are the README conventions for a GitHub repository?
a: Name the file `README.md` at the repo root — GitHub renders it automatically on the repo homepage. Common sections: project title (H1), badges, description, installation, usage, contributing, and license.
fs: `README.md` in the repo root auto-renders on GitHub.
fj: Any filename works; GitHub finds it automatically.

q: How do you link to another heading (anchor link) in the same Markdown file?
a: GitHub auto-generates an anchor for every heading: lowercase the text, replace spaces with `-`, strip special characters. Link to it with `[text](#anchor-id)`. For example, a heading `## My Section` becomes `#my-section`.
fs: Lowercase + hyphenate the heading text for the anchor: `#my-section`.
fj: You need an explicit `<a id='...'>` tag to link to a heading.

q: How do you include a literal backtick inside an inline code span?
a: Use double backticks as the delimiters: ` ``code with `backtick` `` `. You can also use any number of backticks as the delimiter as long as the count differs from what's inside the span.
fs: Double (or more) backtick delimiters let you include backticks inside.
fj: Escape the inner backtick with a backslash: `` `\`` ``.

q: What is the difference between CommonMark and GitHub-Flavored Markdown (GFM)?
a: CommonMark is the base, strictly defined spec. GFM is a strict superset — it adds five extensions: **tables**, **strikethrough**, **task lists**, **extended autolinks**, and **disallowed raw HTML** (script/style/iframe filtering). Any valid CommonMark renders identically under a GFM parser.
fs: GFM = CommonMark + tables, strikethrough, task lists, autolinks, HTML restrictions.
fj: GFM and CommonMark are just different names for the same thing.

followups.q: Why does GFM block certain HTML tags?
followups.a: Tags like `<script>`, `<style>`, and `<iframe>` are filtered server-side on GitHub to prevent XSS attacks when user-generated content is rendered in a browser.
